channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Manufacturing
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] Ransomware halts all U.S. Fairlife production, idling Coca-Cola's ~$4B dairy manufacturing footprint
item.description: Fairlife, Coca-Cola's roughly $4 billion ultra-filtered dairy subsidiary, suspended all U.S. production after a ransomware attack. An SEC filing and Coca-Cola statement confirmed unauthorized third-party access to internal systems “including those related to production”; the company isolated affected networks and shut down portions of operations. Product quality and safety were not impacted, and no restart date was disclosed. Whether the intrusion crossed into OT is unconfirmed — the stoppage appears driven by precautionary IT isolation, the classic pattern where an IT compromise forces OT downtime. For plant managers this is the whole risk in one event: a single ransomware incident idling a national manufacturing footprint, with production halted not because controllers were touched but because the business could not safely prove they weren't. - Cybersecurity Dive / Help Net Security (Coca-Cola SEC filing) - Reported 16 Jul 2026 — production suspended; no restart date disclosed

item.title: [High] Joint advisory: Russia’s FSB Center 16 harvesting router configs — Critical Manufacturing named at risk
item.description: NSA, CISA, the FBI and roughly a dozen international partners issued advisory AA26-194A on Russia’s FSB Center 16 (the actor behind “Berserk Bear”/“Dragonfly”), which harvests configuration data from network devices exposing SNMP or Cisco Smart Install. Critical Manufacturing is a named at-risk sector. For a plant, the exposed gear is the unglamorous edge: line and cell routers, cellular gateways and remote-access appliances left with legacy SNMP or Smart Install reachable. A harvested config is not a breach of your PLCs, but it is a map — VLANs, routes, ACLs and management addresses that describe exactly how your production network is laid out and where the soft joints are. Treat it as reconnaissance groundwork rather than an active OT compromise. - NSA / CISA / FBI + ~12 partners (AA26-194A) - Published 13 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] First in-the-wild exploitation of a PTC Windchill flaw — the PLM system holding your product data
item.description: CISA added CVE-2026-12569, a remote code execution flaw in PTC Windchill, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after the first observed exploitation in the wild. Windchill is PLM software: it holds CAD models, bills of materials, engineering change orders, supplier drawings and the release state of designs. That makes it an unusually rich target and an awkward one to classify — it is not an OT system, but it sits at the junction where engineering intent becomes manufacturing instruction, and it typically integrates with MES and ERP on one side and engineering workstations on the other. The realistic risk for most manufacturers is intellectual-property theft and supply-chain reconnaissance rather than direct process manipulation, but a foothold in PLM is a foothold with excellent visibility into how the plant works. - CVE-2026-12569 - Exploitation reported & KEV addition 26 Jun 2026

item.title: [Critical] SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days exploited — the appliances your OEMs and integrators dial through
item.description: SonicWall confirmed active exploitation of two Secure Mobile Access 1000 flaws after investigating multiple cases. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface; CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2) allows an authenticated administrator to inject OS commands via the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July with a 17 July federal deadline. Manufacturers should read this as a supply-chain access problem rather than a firewall problem. Machine builders, OEMs, robotics vendors and system integrators typically hold standing remote access into cells and lines for diagnostics and warranty support, and that access is frequently brokered through exactly this class of appliance. A compromise there does not trip anomaly detection — it looks like the vendor logging in to check a fault, which is what the vendor does. - CVE-2026-15409 - SonicWall advisory & KEV addition 14 Jul 2026 — reporting 15 Jul; ongoing

item.title: [High] Manufacturing attacks shift toward identity: credential leaks and vishing surge, Doppel reports
item.description: Doppel reports that cyber threats against manufacturing are moving toward identity-driven attacks, with credential leaks and voice phishing both rising. The shift is worth taking seriously because it routes around most of what manufacturers have invested in. Segmentation, firewalls and OT monitoring are built to stop an intruder crossing a boundary; none of them fire when someone signs in with working credentials obtained from a leak or talks a service desk into a password reset. Manufacturing is a soft target for the vishing variant specifically — multi-site operations, shift-based staffing, heavy contractor and agency presence, and a service desk conditioned to restore access fast because downtime is measured in units per hour. Treat identity as a control surface with the same seriousness as the network boundary. - Doppel / Industrial Cyber - Reported 19 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the retrofit boxes on legacy machines
item.description: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038 in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, disclosed in April through the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. On a plant floor these devices are how a 20-year-old press, CNC, test rig or weigh station got a network connection without being replaced — installed during a retrofit, commissioned by an integrator, and then left alone precisely because the machine behind it still works. That combination (unmanaged, unpatched, inside the trusted network, with a clear path to a production asset) is what makes the category attractive. Exploitation is confirmed and follows an explicit OT threat warning. For most manufacturers the first task is discovery: these converters rarely appear in the asset register because nobody procured them as IT equipment. - CVE-2025-67038 - Disclosed Apr 2026 — exploitation reported 25 Jun 2026; ongoing

item.title: [Critical] July ICS Patch Tuesday: Rockwell fixes critical DoS flaws that fault ControlLogix and CompactLogix
item.description: July’s ICS Patch Tuesday is a bad one for plant floors. Rockwell Automation published 12 advisories, including three critical denial-of-service holes in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers that can be exploited to cause a major non-recoverable fault — a controller that stops and stays stopped until someone intervenes. A fourth critical flaw in the 1715 Redundant I/O product lets an unauthenticated attacker reach intrusive CLI commands to read or delete files, stop tasks, change I/O states and modify memory. Siemens added nine advisories, six critical, including a CVSS 10 authentication bypass in Opencenter X and fixes for Simatic S7-1500. For discrete and process manufacturers the exposure is unusually direct: these are not adjacent systems, they are the controllers running the line. - Rockwell PSIRT / Siemens ProductCERT / Schneider / SecurityWeek - Vendor advisories published 14 Jul 2026 (Patch Tuesday) — reporting 15 Jul

item.title: [High] CISA’s 14 July batch: Rockwell 1715-AENTR debug port and two ABB advisories
item.description: CISA issued four ICS advisories on 14 July (ICSA-26-195-01 through -04), released alongside the vendors’ own Patch Tuesday advisories — three from ABB and one from Rockwell. The Rockwell entry covers the 1715-AENTR EtherNet/IP adapter, where a network-accessible debug port fails to enforce proper privilege controls, allowing unauthenticated remote access to intrusive command-line functions. The ABB advisories address Advant Master Online Builder (an incorrect Online Builder version included on installation media) and Ability Edgenius, where a Linux kernel privilege-escalation issue could let a locally authenticated user or a compromised container workload obtain root. Check each advisory for exact affected versions. - ICSA-26-195-01 … -04 - CISA advisories published 14 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] BeyondTrust patches critical auth-bypass flaws in Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access
item.description: BeyondTrust disclosed two authentication-bypass vulnerabilities on 7 July in Remote Support (RS) and Privileged Remote Access (PRA). CVE-2026-40138 (CVSS 9.2) stems from improper validation of authentication data and could allow a network-positioned attacker to bypass access controls and reach the appliance, including elevated accounts; CVE-2026-40139 involves improper processing of RS authentication requests. Both depend on a specific authentication configuration being enabled, which limits the exposed population, and neither has been observed under active exploitation — no public proof-of-concept exists. Cloud customers were patched on 21 April; self-hosted RS/PRA at 25.3.2 or earlier requires action. Read it next to the SonicWall item: privileged remote access is how machine builders and integrators reach your lines, and it is under sustained research attention. - CVE-2026-40138 - Disclosed 7 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] NIST publishes an OT backup quick-start guide focused on cutting recovery time
item.description: NIST released SP 1339, an OT Backup Quick Start Guide aimed at improving industrial cyber resilience and accelerating incident recovery. For manufacturers the framing lands at a useful moment, given this month’s Rockwell advisories describing faults a controller does not recover from on its own (see top story). The artefacts that actually determine how fast a line restarts are PLC logic, HMI projects, robot programs, recipe and setpoint data, drive parameters and the engineering-workstation image needed to push any of it back — not the file server. Those are the things most often stale, undocumented, or stored on the same domain as the systems they would restore. The guide is short-format and pitched at establishing a workable baseline rather than a programme. - NIST SP 1339 - Published 19 Jun 2026

item.title: [Info] Australia’s CISC unveils Enhanced CIRMP Rules covering AI, legacy systems, supply chain and insider risk
item.description: Australia’s Cyber and Infrastructure Security Centre published Enhanced CIRMP Rules addressing AI, legacy systems, supply-chain and insider risks across critical infrastructure. Manufacturers with Australian operations or customers should read the detail, but the wider relevance is the direction: legacy systems and supply-chain risk are moving from acknowledged realities into explicit, written obligations. That is a meaningful shift for a sector whose standard answer on a 20-year-old machine has been that it still runs and cannot be touched. Where regulators name legacy assets and insider risk directly, “we accepted it” starts requiring an artefact rather than a conversation — and the same expectations tend to migrate into customer security questionnaires well before they migrate into other jurisdictions’ law. - CISC / Industrial Cyber - Published 18 Jun 2026
